mainmatter.p: Let X be a displayed category over B and let Y be a displayed category over C, and let { X } \xrightarrow [ F ]{ \bar F }{ Y } and { X } \xrightarrow [ G ]{ \bar G }{ Y } be two displayed functors over { B } \xrightarrow{F , G} { C } respectively. Given a natural transformation { F } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { G }, a displayed natural transformation from \bar F to \bar G over \alpha, written { \bar F } \xrightarrow [ \alpha ]{ \bar \alpha }{ \bar G }, is defined by the following data:

ol.li: for each displayed object x \in X ^b, an assigned displayed morphism { \bar F {x} } \xrightarrow [ \alpha _b ]{ \bar \alpha _x }{ \bar G {x} };
such that for each { x } \xrightarrow [ f ]{ \bar{f} }{ y } in X, we have \alpha _x; \bar G \bar{f} = \bar F \bar{f} ; \alpha _y.

mainmatter.p: We will develop the theory of functors between displayed categories or fibrations as well as natural transformations between these functors, building on Ahrens and Lumsdaine and Jacobs.

Let X be a displayed category over B and let Y be a displayed category over C, and let { B } \xrightarrow{F} { C } be a functor. A displayed functor from X to Y over F, written { X } \xrightarrow [ F ]{ \bar F }{ Y } is defined by the following data:

ol.li: for each displayed object x \in X ^b, an assigned object \bar{F} x \in Y ^{ F b};
for each displayed morphism { x } \xrightarrow [ f ]{ \bar{f} }{ y } in X, an assigned morphism { \bar F {x} } \xrightarrow [ F {f} ]{ \bar F \bar{f} }{ \bar F y };
such that \bar F 1 _{ x } = 1 _{ \bar F x } and \bar F { \left ( \bar{f} ; \bar{g} \right )} = \bar F {f}; \bar F {g}.

mainmatter.p: Let X be a displayed category over B and let Y be a displayed category over C, and let { B } \xrightarrow{F} { C } be a functor. A displayed functor from X to Y over F is called fibered when it preserves cartesian morphisms.

Let X be a displayed category over B and let Y be a displayed category over C, and let { X } \xrightarrow [ F ]{ \bar F }{ Y } and { X } \xrightarrow [ G ]{ \bar G }{ Y } be two displayed functors over { B } \xrightarrow{F , G} { C } respectively. Given a natural transformation { F } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { G }, a displayed natural transformation from \bar F to \bar G over \alpha, written { \bar F } \xrightarrow [ \alpha ]{ \bar \alpha }{ \bar G }, is defined by the following data:

ol.li: for each displayed object x \in X ^b, an assigned displayed morphism { \bar F {x} } \xrightarrow [ \alpha _b ]{ \bar \alpha _x }{ \bar G {x} };
such that for each { x } \xrightarrow [ f ]{ \bar{f} }{ y } in X, we have \alpha _x; \bar G \bar{f} = \bar F \bar{f} ; \alpha _y.

mainmatter.p: Let B be a category. A displayed category E over B is defined by the following data according to (Ahrens and Lumsdaine):

ol.li: for each object x \in B, a collection of displayed objects E _{ x },

for each morphism { x } \xrightarrow{f} { y } \in B and displayed objects \bar{x} \in E _{ x } and \bar{y} \in E _{ y }, a family of collections of displayed morphisms \mathbf{hom} _{ E _{ f } } { \left ( \bar{x} , \bar{y} \right )}, an element of which shall denote by { \bar{x} } \xrightarrow [ f ]{ \bar{f} }{ \bar{y} },
for each x \in B and \bar{x} \in E _{ x }, a displayed morphism { \bar{x} } \xrightarrow [ 1 _{ x } ]{ 1 _{ \bar{x} } }{ \bar{x} },

for each { x } \xrightarrow{f} { y } and { y } \xrightarrow{g} { z } in B and objects \bar{x} \in E _{ x } , \bar{y} \in E _{ y } , \bar{z} \in E _{ z }, a function \mathbf{hom} _{ E _{ f } } { \left ( \bar{x} , \bar{y} \right )} \times \mathbf{hom} _{ E _{ g } } { \left ( \bar{y} , \bar{z} \right )} \to \mathbf{hom} _{ E _{ f;g } } { \left ( \bar{x} , \bar{z} \right )} that we will denote like ordinary (diagrammatic) function composition,

such that the following equations hold: 1 _{ \bar{x} } ; \bar{h} = \bar{h} \qquad \bar{h} ; 1 _{ \bar{y} } = \bar{h} \qquad \bar{f} ;( \bar{g} ; \bar{h} ) = ( \bar{f} ; \bar{g} ); \bar{h} Note that these are well-defined because of the corresponding laws for the base category B.

mainmatter.p: When we have too many subscripts, we will write E[x] instead of E _{ x }.
